title: How to make a conservatory cheaper to heat
description: Conservatories leak heat through the roof and glazing. The fixes that actually work, what roof insulation costs, and what heating one really costs per hour.
blurb: Why conservatories lose heat so fast, the cheap fixes worth doing first, and when roof insulation pays for itself.
lede: A conservatory is, structurally speaking, a greenhouse bolted to your house. That is wonderful in April and miserable in January, when the same glazing that traps spring sunshine dumps your expensive heat straight into the sky. Conservatory walls and roofs lose heat between five and fifteen times faster than an insulated brick wall, which is why so many stand empty from November to March with the door firmly shut. You cannot turn one into a snug room for the price of a draught excluder, but there is a sensible ladder of fixes, from a £15 film kit to a full insulated roof, and knowing which rung gives what is the difference between money well spent and money evaporated through polycarbonate.
short_answer: Heating an uninsulated conservatory with a 2 kW electric heater for three hours a day costs about £47 a month at the current cap, and most of that heat leaves through the roof. The cheapest worthwhile fixes are draught-proofing, thermal blinds or curtains, and a rug over a tiled floor, all for under £200 combined. The single biggest improvement is insulating or replacing the roof, which typically costs £2,500 to £5,000 for an internal insulated ceiling and £5,000 to £12,000 for a solid tiled replacement.

sections.h2: Why a conservatory is so expensive to heat
sections.html:
Heat loss is governed by U-values: the lower the number, the slower heat escapes through a surface. An insulated cavity wall sits around 0.3 to 0.5 W/m²K and a well-insulated loft around 0.16. Compare that with what a conservatory is made of:

  • Single glazing: roughly 5.7 W/m²K, the worst surface in any home.
  • Older double glazing: around 2.8 to 3.1.
  • 16 mm polycarbonate roof sheets: around 2.3 to 2.5, and they also leak air at every joint.
  • Modern double-glazed units: around 1.2 to 1.6, better, but still four to eight times leakier than an insulated wall.

Then there is geometry. A conservatory is almost all external surface: three glazed walls, a glazed or polycarbonate roof, and often an uninsulated concrete slab with tiles straight on top. The roof is usually the biggest single loser because warm air rises and polycarbonate offers almost no resistance. On a clear winter night the roof also radiates heat directly to the sky, which is why conservatories feel colder than the air temperature suggests.

The practical consequence: any heat you pump in leaves within minutes of the heater switching off. That changes the strategy. In the rest of the house you heat the fabric and it stays warm; in a conservatory you are mostly heating the garden unless you fix the fabric first. The same logic that makes loft insulation the best-value job in a normal house applies here with the volume turned up: deal with the roof, then the gaps, then think about heating.

sections.h2: The cheap fixes: under £200, do these first
sections.html:
None of these turn a conservatory into a living room, but together they take the edge off and they cost little enough that payback is quick if you use the room at all in winter.

  • Draught-proofing (£10 to £40). Conservatory doors, the door between house and conservatory, and the joints where frames meet brickwork are all common leak points. Self-adhesive seals and a tube of frame sealant deal with most of it. Our draught-proofing guide covers the materials and where to put them.
  • Thermal blinds or curtains (£50 to £150). A layer of still air trapped against glazing roughly halves its heat loss overnight. Honeycomb (cellular) blinds are the best performers for glazed walls; for the wall between house and conservatory, a heavy curtain over the connecting door helps both rooms. The physics is the same as for windows generally, covered in curtains for warmth.
  • Secondary glazing film (£10 to £20 a kit). Shrink-fit film across single-glazed panels adds a trapped air layer for pennies per pane. It is a winter-only measure and you will not open those windows, but on an old single-glazed conservatory it is the best £15 you can spend.
  • A rug or carpet offcut (£20 to £60). Tiles over an uninsulated slab pull heat out of your feet all day. A thick rug fixes the felt temperature far more than its insulation value suggests.
  • Reflectors behind any radiator (under £15). If the conservatory has a radiator on its one solid dwarf wall, a foil reflector stops it warming the brickwork behind.

Realistic expectation: together these might cut heat loss by 15 to 25 per cent. They will not make a polycarbonate-roofed conservatory hold heat overnight. For that you need the roof.

sections.h2: Insulating the roof: the job that actually changes the room
sections.html:
The roof is where serious money goes and where serious results come from. There are three main routes, in ascending order of cost and effect:

1. Insulated panels in the existing glazing bars (£1,000 to £2,500)

Rigid insulated panels replace the polycarbonate sheets in the existing roof structure. Quick to fit, no structural work, and it keeps the conservatory legally a conservatory. You lose the light from above and gain a roof several times better than polycarbonate, though the glazing bars themselves still conduct heat and the result is well short of a proper insulated roof.

2. An internal insulated ceiling (£2,500 to £5,000 for a typical 3.5 m by 3.5 m room)

A frame is built under the existing roof, filled with insulation (usually multifoil or rigid board), vapour-sealed and plasterboarded. Done properly this transforms the room: the ceiling U-value drops to something approaching a habitable room and the conservatory stops behaving like a greenhouse in both directions, so it is cooler in summer too. The phrase done properly matters. The insulation must be continuous, the vapour barrier sealed, and a ventilation gap maintained above it, otherwise warm moist air condenses on the cold polycarbonate above and drips back into your new ceiling. Use an installer who will explain their condensation control, not just their price.

3. A solid replacement roof (£5,000 to £12,000)

The polycarbonate or glass roof comes off entirely and a lightweight insulated tiled roof goes on. This is the full conversion: the room becomes usable year round and the roof performs like the rest of the house. It is also the point at which building regulations definitely apply, because you are changing the structure. The frames and footings must be checked to confirm they can carry the new roof, and the work needs building control sign-off. Budget at the top of the range if you want roof windows to keep some overhead light, which most people end up wanting.

Prices above are typical quoted ranges for an average UK conservatory in 2026; get at least three quotes because this market has wide spreads for identical work. If your conservatory has cavity dwarf walls, it is worth checking whether they were ever filled at the same time, since the principles in our cavity wall insulation guide apply to those short walls too.

sections.h2: What heating a conservatory actually costs
sections.html:
Because most conservatories cannot legally be on the main central heating loop (more on that below), they are usually heated with plug-in electric heaters. Electricity costs roughly three and a half times more per unit than gas, so this is the most expensive heat in your house going into the leakiest room in your house.

HeaterTypical powerCost per hourCost per month (3 h/day)
Fan heater on full2 kW52p£47.00
Oil-filled radiator1.5 kW39p£35.25
Halogen or radiant bar1.2 kW31p£28.20
Panel heater1 kW26p£23.50

At 26.11p per kWh, Ofgem price cap July to September 2026. Monthly figures assume 30 days and the heater drawing full power throughout, which is realistic in an uninsulated conservatory because the thermostat rarely gets a rest.

Two things follow from that table. First, in an uninsulated conservatory a thermostat barely helps, because the room loses heat as fast as a 2 kW heater supplies it; the heater simply runs flat out. After roof insulation the same heater cycles on and off and the real monthly cost can drop by half or more for the same comfort. Second, heater choice matters less than fabric: the differences between heater types are pennies per hour, while the difference between an insulated and uninsulated roof is pounds per day. If you do want to compare heater types properly, see electric heater types compared.

One honest rule of thumb: if you only use the conservatory occasionally in winter, a radiant heater pointed at the seating is the cheapest option, because it warms people rather than trying to warm an unwinnable volume of air.

sections.h2: Central heating, building regulations and the door rule
sections.html:
This is the part most guides skip. In England and Wales, a conservatory is exempt from building regulations only while it meets some conditions: it is under 30 m², separated from the house by external-quality doors, glazed to the usual safety standards, and any heating in it is independent of the main system with its own controls. The thermal separation is the important one for energy. The exemption exists precisely because a conservatory is assumed to be a leaky bolt-on that you can shut off from the heated house.

Run a radiator from your main central heating into the conservatory, or take the connecting doors out, and the exemption falls away: the conservatory is then treated as an extension and must meet the full insulation standards of one, which a glazed structure cannot. Plenty of older conservatories have a teed-off radiator anyway, but know the position before paying for one, and give any new connection its own thermostatic control.

The door rule also carries the single most effective free energy measure in this whole article: keep the doors between house and conservatory closed in winter. An open-plan knock-through into an unheated glass box couples your warm living room to the coldest space in the property, and your boiler pays for it all season. Check the current rules for your nation on the official planning portal before any roof replacement or heating change, since the details differ in Scotland and Northern Ireland and the thresholds get revised.

Work up the ladder and stop at the rung that matches how you use the room. If the conservatory is a three-season room and you accept it goes dormant in deep winter, spend under £200 on draughts, blinds, film and a rug, keep the doors to the house shut, and use a radiant heater on the rare cold days you sit out there. If you want a genuine year-round room, the roof is the job: an internal insulated ceiling from around £2,500, or a solid replacement roof from around £5,000, with everything else as supporting cast. What does not make sense is the middle path many households drift into, which is running a 2 kW heater for hours each day in an unimproved conservatory. At £47 a month that habit costs £150 to £250 over a winter, every winter, and buys you a room that still feels cold. Two or three winters of that money is a deposit on a roof that fixes the problem for good.

faq.q: Can I put a radiator in my conservatory?
faq.a: Yes, but in England and Wales a conservatory only stays exempt from building regulations if any heating in it is independent of the main system, with its own on/off and temperature controls. Extending the central heating loop into it, or removing the doors that separate it from the house, can mean the whole structure has to comply with extension standards. Many people fit a plug-in electric radiator instead, which keeps things simple. Check the current rules on the government planning portal before commissioning work.

faq.q: How much does conservatory roof insulation cost in the UK?
faq.a: Typical 2026 prices for an average conservatory: £1,000 to £2,500 for insulated panels fitted into the existing glazing bars, £2,500 to £5,000 for an internal insulated and plasterboarded ceiling, and £5,000 to £12,000 for a full lightweight tiled replacement roof. Quotes vary widely for identical work, so get at least three.

faq.q: Will insulating my conservatory roof cause condensation?
faq.a: It can if the job is done badly. Warm moist air that gets past the new ceiling condenses on the cold polycarbonate or glass above it. A proper installation includes a sealed vapour barrier on the warm side and a ventilation path above the insulation. Ask any installer to explain their condensation control before you sign; if the answer is vague, choose another installer.

faq.q: Is it cheaper to heat a conservatory with gas or electric?
faq.a: Gas heat costs roughly 8 to 9p per useful kWh through a modern boiler against 26.11p per kWh for electricity at the July to September 2026 cap, so gas is much cheaper per unit. But building regulations push most conservatories towards independent electric heating, and in an uninsulated conservatory the fuel matters less than the fabric, because most of the heat is lost within minutes either way. Insulate first, then worry about the heat source.

faq.q: What is the cheapest way to use a conservatory in winter without big spending?
faq.a: Keep the doors to the house closed, draught-proof the frames and doors, fit honeycomb blinds or use shrink-fit window film on single glazing, put a thick rug over the tiled floor, and use a radiant heater aimed at where you sit rather than trying to heat the whole air volume. All of that comes in under £200 and makes occasional winter use bearable, though it will not make the room hold heat overnight.
